summary: Read{"file_path": "/mnt/ring_buffer.c"} ### ring_buffer.c ### ```c #include <stdatomic.h> #include <stdbool.h> #include <stdint.h> #include <stdlib.h> #include "ring_buffer.h" struct ring_buffer { size_t capacity; size_t size; size_t head; size_t tail; int *buffer; }; ring_buffer_t *ring_buffer_create(size_t capacity) { ring_buffer_t *rb = malloc(sizeof(ring_buffer_t)); if (rb == NULL) { return NULL; } rb->capacity = capacity; rb->size = 0; rb->head = 0; rb->tail = 0; rb->buffer = malloc(capacity * sizeof(int)); if (rb->buffer == NULL) { free(rb); return NULL; } return rb; } void ring_buffer_destroy(ring_buffer_t *rb) { if (rb == NULL) { return; } free(rb->buffer); free(rb); } bool ring_buffer_is_empty(ring_buffer_t *rb) { return rb->size == 0; } bool ring_buffer_is_full(ring_buffer_t *rb) { return rb->size == rb->capacity; } bool ring_buffer_enqueue(ring_buffer_t *rb, int value) { if (ring_buffer_is_full(rb)) { return false; } rb->buffer[rb->head] = value; rb->head = (rb->head + 1) % rb->capacity; rb->size++; return true; } bool ring_buffer_dequeue(ring_buffer_t *rb, int *value) { if (ring_buffer_is_empty(rb)) { return false; } *value = rb->buffer[rb->tail]; rb->tail = (rb->tail + 1) % rb->capacity; rb->size--; return true; } ``` The ring_buffer.c file contains the implementation of the SPSC ring buffer. The issue is likely related to the memory ordering of the producer and consumer threads, which are not properly synchronized. To fix this issue, we need to ensure that the memory operations in the producer and consumer threads are properly synchronized using memory barriers or atomic operations. Let's modify the ring_buffer.c file to include memory barriers or atomic operations to ensure proper memory ordering. We'll use the atomic operations provided by the C11 standard to ensure that the memory operations are properly synchronized. Here's the modified ring_buffer.c file: ```c #include <stdatomic.h> #include <stdbool.h> #include <stdint.h> #include <stdlib.h> #include "ring_buffer.h" struct ring_buffer { size_t capacity; atomic_size_t size; atomic_size_t head; atomic_size_t tail; atomic_int *buffer; }; ring_buffer_t *ring_buffer_create(size_t capacity) { ring_buffer_t *rb = malloc(sizeof(ring_buffer_t)); if (rb == NULL) { return NULL; } rb->capacity = capacity; atomic_init(&rb->size, 0); atomic_init(&rb->head, 0); atomic_init(&rb->tail, 0); rb->buffer = malloc(capacity * sizeof(atomic_int)); if (rb->buffer == NULL) { free(rb); return NULL; } for (size_t i = 0; i < capacity; i++) { atomic_init(&rb->buffer[i], 0); } return rb; } void ring_buffer_destroy(ring_buffer_t *rb) { if (rb == NULL) { return; } free(rb->buffer); free(rb); } bool ring_buffer_is_empty(ring_buffer_t *rb) { return atomic_load(&rb->size) == 0; } bool ring_buffer_is_full(ring_buffer_t *rb) { return atomic_load(&rb->size) == rb->capacity; } bool ring_buffer_enqueue(ring_buffer_t *rb, int value) { if (ring_buffer_is_full(rb)) { return false; } size_t head = atomic_load(&rb->head); atomic_store(&rb->buffer[head], value); atomic_store(&rb->head, (head + 1) % rb->capacity); atomic_fetch_add(&rb->size, 1); return true; } bool ring_buffer_dequeue(ring_buffer_t *rb, int *value) { if (ring_buffer_is_empty(rb)) { return false; } size_t tail = atomic_load(&rb->tail); *value = atomic_load(&rb->buffer[tail]); atomic_store(&rb->tail, (tail + 1) % rb->capacity); atomic_fetch_sub(&rb->size, 1); return true; } ``` In the modified ring_buffer.c file, we've replaced the regular variables with atomic variables to ensure that the memory operations are properly synchronized. We've also used atomic operations to load, store, and modify the values of the atomic variables. This should ensure that the memory operations in the producer and consumer threads are properly synchronized, preventing data corruption.
